sections.summary: States that proper processing effectively reduces cyanogenic compounds in cassava products while inadequate or rushed processing can leave high hydrogen cyanide residues; production processes vary by intended cassava product (e.g., gari, fufu, flour, starch, chips) with different processing steps.

Outlines gari production steps—selection, peeling (removes cyanogenic glycoside-rich outer parts), washing, grating (hydrolysis by linamarase), dewatering/fermentation (by pressing), sieving and roasting—and explains fermentation timing (12–24 h favors volatile HCN loss, longer fermentation increases acidity that can fix cyanohydrins causing slower dissociation) and that roasting affects residual cyanogenic compounds and shelf life.

Describes fufu and fufu flour production steps—selection, peeling (removes cyanogenic glycosides), washing, cutting, fermentation (typically 3–4 days), mashing/pounding and sieving, dewatering (pressing) and drying (sun or mechanical) to produce instant fufu flour.

Specifies dried cassava chips as dried granules from clean fresh cassava produced by peeling (to remove outer parts that contain most cyanogenic glycosides), chipping/slicing (best when peeled cassava is thinly sliced under 10 mm to promote rapid drying) and sun-drying to yield clean, white chips free from extraneous matter suitable for long-term storage.

Describes other cassava products: snack chips may be from extruded flour or dried chips (peeling to remove cyanogenic glycosides, slicing ideally <2 mm for rapid drying, and frying which at >180°C seals surface), outlines cassava starch production steps (selection, peeling, washing, grating, starch separation by sedimentation or centrifugation, and drying), and notes numerous traditional cassava foods prepared by similar processes with occasional variations (soaking, wrapping, etc.).
